title: Te Arotakenga Pānga Taiao o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | The Mangakōtukutuku Stream Environmental Impact Assessment
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream that flows through our rohe and holds deep cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream is named for the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks and provided medicinal resources for our tūpuna. Students will learn how traditional knowledge of stream health indicators connects to modern scientific assessment methods.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data (pH, turbidity, macroinvertebrates) from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific equipment and methods
Compare traditional Māori indicators of stream health (e.g., presence of tuna, clarity of water) with scientific measurements to identify correlations
Propose evidence-based recommendations for improving the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku that integrate both mātauranga Māori and Western science
Present findings to peers and community members using appropriate scientific terminology and cultural protocols

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia at the streamside. A local kaumātua shares the pūrākau (story) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students make initial observations of the stream environment, noting both cultural indicators (presence of significant species, water clarity) and potential human impacts.

activities.title: He Tātari Pūnaha - Systematic Testing
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students collect and record quantitative data: pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and temperature at three designated sites along the stream. They practice proper scientific method, ensuring fair testing and accurate recording.

activities.title: Kimihia ngā Kōura - Macroinvertebrate Survey
activities.description: Students conduct a 'stream kick' sample to collect macroinvertebrates, using identification charts to classify species. They calculate a Macroinvertebrate Community Index (MCI) score, a key scientific indicator of stream health, and compare the results to the cultural knowledge shared by the kaumātua.

activities.title: He Kohikohinga Raraunga, He Tātari Raraunga - Data Collation and Analysis
activities.description: Back in the lab, groups collate all class data. They create graphs and charts to visualize the health of the stream at the different sites. Students analyze the data to identify trends, correlations, and potential causes for any issues found, linking scientific data to the cultural narrative of the stream.

activities.title: Te Whakatau, te Whakamānawa - Conclusion and Presentation
activities.description: Student groups synthesize their findings to prepare a brief presentation. They must state the overall health of the stream, supported by their evidence, and propose one actionable recommendation for its protection (kaitiakitanga). They present their findings to the class and a invited community panel, opening with a mihi.

resources: Local Kaumātua (e.g., Uncle Wiremu Heihei) for cultural narrative and traditional ecological knowledge
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources and identification guides
Water testing kits for pH, nitrates, turbidity, and temperature
Mangakōtukutuku College's historical water quality data records for longitudinal comparison

tasks: Accurate and neatly recorded field data booklet
Correctly calculated MCI score and interpretation
A collaboratively created graph or chart visualizing a key data set
A 3-minute group presentation proposing an evidence-based action for stream improvement

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
Planet Earth and Beyond: Interacting systems - Investigate the external and internal processes that shape and change the landscape
